title: What Makes Restaurants Click? A Data Mining Study Using Online Delivery Data
text:
1 Introduction
The emergence of online food delivery platforms such as Zomato and Swiggy has transformed the restaurant industry, making data crucial for understanding customer preferences and operational efficiency. These platforms generate rich data capturing both restaurant characteristics and delivery dynamics.
This study uses two complementary datasets: one focusing on restaurant attributes such as ratings, cost, cuisine, and menu details, and the other on delivery-related factors including personnel details, location coordinates, timestamps, weather, traffic, and delivery time.
By integrating these datasets, the study applies data mining techniques such as exploratory analysis, clustering, regression, and classification to identify patterns that differentiate successful restaurants from underperforming ones.
The objective is to derive data-driven insights into the key factors that determine whether a restaurant “clicks” or “flops” in a competitive online ecosystem.



2 Problem Statement
Despite the availability of extensive data from food delivery platforms, many restaurants struggle to understand the key factors that influence their success. High ratings do not always translate into higher order volumes, and efficient delivery does not always guarantee customer satisfaction.
The core problem addressed in this study is:

To identify and model the critical factors that determine restaurant success or failure by analyzing both restaurant characteristics and delivery performance using data mining techniques.

This includes understanding:

Which restaurant features (e.g., cost, cuisine, ratings) most strongly influence customer preference
How delivery-related factors (e.g., time taken, traffic, weather) impact overall satisfaction




3 Objectives
The main objectives of this study are:

3.0.1 Data Understanding and Preprocessing

Clean and preprocess both datasets (handle missing values, inconsistent timestamps, categorical encoding)
Integrate restaurant and delivery datasets where applicable



3.0.2 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

Analyze distributions of ratings, cost, cuisine types, and delivery times
Identify trends across cities and customer behavior patterns
Examine relationships between variables such as rating vs cost, delivery time vs traffic



3.0.3 Feature Analysis

Determine the most influential features affecting restaurant success
Evaluate the impact of delivery conditions (weather, traffic, vehicle condition) on time taken



3.0.4 Predictive Modeling

Build models to predict:

Restaurant success (e.g., high vs low rating/popularity)
Delivery time based on operational factors

Apply techniques such as regression and classification



3.0.5 Pattern Discovery

Use clustering to group restaurants based on performance and characteristics
Identify hidden patterns linking cuisine, pricing, and customer ratings



3.0.6 Insight Generation

Provide actionable recommendations for:

Restaurant owners (pricing, cuisine strategy, service improvement)
Delivery platforms (optimization of logistics and recommendations)

title: Phase 3 - Model Building and Evaluation
text: This phase builds regression models to predict delivery time and compares their performance. To keep the workflow stable and fast for this project, the final comparison uses Linear Regression and Decision Tree Regression.

title: Phase 1 - Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
text:
5 Final variables


Code
tribble(
  ~Variable, ~Purpose,
  "time_taken_min", "Target variable: delivery time in minutes",
  "distance_km", "Engineered Haversine distance between restaurant and destination",
  "pickup_delay_min", "Time gap between order placement and pickup",
  "order_hour", "Hour of order",
  "order_day_of_week", "Day of week extracted from order date",
  "order_month", "Month extracted from order date",
  "is_weekend", "Weekend indicator",
  "age_group", "Binned delivery partner age",
  "is_rush_hour", "Peak-time indicator",
  "road_traffic_density_ord", "Ordinal traffic encoding"
) |>
  kbl(caption = "Important processed and engineered variables") |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

title: Phase 1 - Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
text:
6 Missing values after preprocessing


Code
missing_summary <- df |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "column", values_to = "missing_count") |>
  mutate(missing_pct = round(100 * missing_count / nrow(df), 2)) |>
  arrange(desc(missing_count))

missing_summary |>
  filter(missing_count > 0) |>
  kbl(caption = "Remaining missing values after preprocessing") |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE)

title: Phase 1 - Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
text:
8 Distribution of engineered features

8.1 Distance


Code
ggplot(df, aes(x = distance_km)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#01696f", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Delivery Distance", x = "Distance (km)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)











8.2 Pickup delay


Code
ggplot(df, aes(x = pickup_delay_min)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#a12c7b", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Pickup Delay", x = "Pickup Delay (min)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Phase 1 - Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
text:
9 Key preprocessing notes

Raw IDs were retained in the cleaned dataset but excluded later from modelling.
Traffic was ordinally encoded because it has natural order: Low < Medium < High < Jam.
Time-based features were created from date and order/pickup timestamps.
Distance was engineered from latitude and longitude because it is more meaningful than raw coordinates alone.

title: Phase 2 - Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
5 Summary statistics


Code
df |>
  summarise(
    mean_time = mean(time_taken_min, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_time = median(time_taken_min, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_time = min(time_taken_min, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_time = max(time_taken_min, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_time = sd(time_taken_min, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_distance = mean(distance_km, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_pickup_delay = mean(pickup_delay_min, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  kbl(caption = "Summary of key numeric variables") |>
  kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

title: Phase 2 - Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
7 Distribution of delivery time


Code
ggplot(df, aes(x = time_taken_min)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#01696f", color = "white") +
  geom_density(aes(y = after_stat(count)), color = "#a12c7b", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Delivery Time",
    x = "Time Taken (min)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Phase 2 - Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
8 Correlation heatmap


Code
num_df <- df |>
  select(where(is.numeric)) |>
  select(-any_of("id"))

cor_mat <- cor(num_df, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

cor_long <- as.data.frame(cor_mat) |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("var1") |>
  pivot_longer(-var1, names_to = "var2", values_to = "correlation")

ggplot(cor_long, aes(var1, var2, fill = correlation)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#b2182b", mid = "white", high = "#01696f", midpoint = 0) +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(
    title = "Correlation Heatmap of Numeric Variables",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Corr"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

title: Phase 2 - Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
9 Distance versus time


Code
ggplot(df, aes(x = distance_km, y = time_taken_min)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, color = "#01696f") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#a12c7b") +
  labs(
    title = "Delivery Time vs Distance",
    x = "Distance (km)",
    y = "Time Taken (min)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)


`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

  labs(
    title = "Delivery Time by Traffic Density",
    x = "Traffic Density",
    y = "Time Taken (min)"

  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Phase 2 - Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
17 Key observations

Delivery time generally increases with distance.
Heavy traffic and jam conditions increase delivery time more than low traffic.
Pickup delay also contributes directly to final delivery time.
Weather and city category create visible differences in delivery performance.

title: Phase 3 - Model Building and Evaluation
text: This phase builds regression models to predict delivery time and compares their performance. To keep the workflow stable and fast for this project, the final comparison uses Linear Regression and Decision Tree Regression.

title: Phase 3 - Model Building and Evaluation
text:
9 Interpretation

Linear Regression provides a simple baseline and shows the direction of feature effects.
Decision Tree captures non-linear patterns better than a purely linear model.
The better model is the one with lower RMSE and MAE and higher R2.
If both models are close, Linear Regression is easier to explain in an academic report.

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
11 Conclusion
This modeling project demonstrated how simple regression and tree methods can provide actionable insights for a food delivery business. Even with basic models, it’s possible to achieve solid predictive performance while identifying the main bottlenecks in operational efficiency.

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
1 Business Context
Timely delivery is the most critical factor for customer satisfaction in food delivery services. Delays can directly impact customer retention, partner ratings, and cost efficiency.
In this analysis, we aim to predict the delivery time (in minutes) for an online food delivery platform using past data from multiple cities.
The objective is twofold: - Build predictive models that can estimate the expected delivery time for new orders.
- Identify key factors that most influence the delivery time — such as traffic, weather, and order conditions.

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
2 Key Questions
This modeling project intends to answer: 1. Can we predict delivery time before the order starts?
2. Which factors most strongly influence delivery time?
3. How do simple models like Linear Regression compare to Decision Trees in performance?
4. What conditions lead to higher or lower delivery times (e.g., weekends, rush hours, poor weather)?

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
3 Data Overview
The cleaned dataset food_delivery_clean.csv contains various attributes describing each order and its delivery conditions.
Sample Features: - time_taken_min: Dependent variable (target) - distance_km: Distance from restaurant to customer - pickup_delay_min: Time delay before pickup - order_hour: Hour of order - is_weekend, is_rush_hour: Time-based indicators - delivery_person_age, delivery_person_ratings, vehicle_condition - road_traffic_density_ord, weather_conditions, city, etc.


Code
df <- read_csv("../output/food_delivery_clean.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
df |>
  glimpse()


Rows: 40,036
Columns: 29

After cleaning, categorical variables were one-hot encoded and missing values removed, leaving a modeling dataset ready for regression tasks.

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
4 Data Splitting and Preparation
We divided the dataset into: - Training set: 80% for model training and cross-validation
- Testing set: 20% for unbiased evaluation

4.1 Data Partition Summary


Code
train_rows <- floor(0.8 * nrow(df))
cat("Training rows:", train_rows, "| Testing rows:", nrow(df) - train_rows)


Training rows: 32028 | Testing rows: 8008

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
5 Modeling Approach
Two models were trained using 5-fold cross-validation:

Linear Regression (lm) — interpretable and baseline model.

Decision Tree (rpart) — non-linear, handles feature interactions automatically.

Both models predict time_taken_min.

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
6 Model Performance
The comparison below shows how well each model performed on unseen test data.

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
7 Model Interpretations

7.1 Linear Regression (Baseline)
The linear model assumes a linear relationship between predictors and delivery time.










The dashed line represents perfect predictions.
Scatter spread indicates the degree of model error.


7.1.1 Residual Analysis
Residual plots help check whether the model systematically over- or under-predicts.









The linear model shows some patterning — suggesting the relationships might not be strictly linear.




7.2 Decision Tree Model
Decision Trees can capture non-linear splits (e.g., traffic × distance interactions).










7.2.1 Residuals









Decision trees sometimes overfit to localized data but can better capture extreme cases compared to linear models.

text:

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
9 Insights Summary







Finding
Interpretation




Distance & traffic
Long distances and higher traffic levels significantly increase delivery times.


Pickup delay
Time spent before a rider starts moving contributes directly to total delay.


Ratings & vehicle condition
Better-rated riders and better vehicles tend to deliver faster.


Weekend & rush hour
Weekends and rush hours generally show longer delivery times.


Model comparison
Decision Tree slightly improves prediction accuracy over Linear Regression, but at the cost of interpretability.

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
10 Recommendations
Based on this modeling exercise, the food delivery platform could: - Prioritize high-rated riders for long or high-traffic deliveries. - Optimize dispatch time to reduce pickup delays. - Prepare staffing strategies for weekend/rush-hour surges. - Explore advanced models (e.g., Random Forest, XGBoost) for higher predictive accuracy.

title: Phase 3 - Modelling the dataset
text:
11 11. Next Steps
For future iterations: - Include real-time weather and traffic APIs to improve prediction quality.
- Add feature scaling and regularization (ridge/lasso) for linear models.
- Analyze feature interactions using SHAP or variable importance plots for tree models.
